notes.anchor: He has been ordered to lead the cavalry from Ambracia through Thessaly
notes.note: Young Marcus Cicero was serving as a cavalry officer under Brutus in Greece; Ambracia was a port city in Epirus, and the route through Thessaly led northward into Macedonia where Brutus was concentrating his army.

Glyco was the personal physician who attended Gaius Vibius Pansa as he lay dying after the battle of Forum Gallorum (April 43 BC); rumours circulated — apparently fomented by partisans of Octavian — that he had poisoned his patient.

notes.anchor: is being held in custody as a parricide
notes.note: "Parricide" (parricidium) in Roman law covered not only killing a parent but any murder of a person in a position of quasi-paternal authority; a physician accused of killing his patient could be charged under this heading.

notes.anchor: Satrius, the lieutenant of C. Trebonius
notes.note: Gaius Trebonius, one of the Liberators (consul 45 BC), was murdered by Dolabella at Smyrna in early 43 BC; the dispatch apparently brought earlier intelligence of Dolabella's defeat in Asia, which Trebonius's surviving lieutenants relayed westward.

notes.anchor: Dolabella has been cut down and put to flight
notes.note: This preliminary report of Dolabella's defeat proved premature or partial; he was not definitively cornered until later in 43 BC, when he was besieged at Laodicea by Cassius and took his own life.

notes.anchor: 19 May, from camp at the foot of Candavia
notes.note: The Candavian range (modern Albanian highlands) lies on the Via Egnatia east of Dyrrachium; Brutus's army was moving eastward through Epirus and Macedonia, and this dateline places him on the Adriatic side of that mountain barrier.
